coredata.dc:title: Bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots as selective nanoprobe for detection of 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol in aqueous medium
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ashokan, Indhumathi
Bhunia, Susanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Different types of phenolic pollutants pose a major risk to the environment and the health of living things. It is therefore crucial for their selective recognition especially nitrophenols. In this work, yellow fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were used as selective nanoprobe to efficiently detect 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). One-pot synthetic method was adopted for making C-dots from o-phenylenediamine and oleic acid mixture. The synthesized C-dots showed an excitation wavelength of 420 nm with Stokes shift of up to 135 nm. These C-dots exhibited instantaneous selective detection of 2-NP and 4-NP in aqueous solution with a detection limit of 6.4 nM and 4.8 nM respectively. Upon incubated with 2-NP and 4-NP, the C-dots’ fluorescence underwent quenching with a maximum extent of respective 80 % and 83 %. By comparing with other analogs of analyte, it was discovered that the emission intensity of the C-dots remained unchanged. Static quenching was proposed behind the changes of fluorescence after the treatment with 2-NP and 4-NP. Such type of C-dots will demonstrate the selective sensing platform in real field application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bright yellow fluorescence
Selective nitrophenol detection
Static quenching

Bhunia expresses his appreciation to \u201C VIT SEED GRANT \u201D and DST INSPIRE funding (DST/ INSPIRE Faculty Award /2018/DST/INSPIRE/04/2018/002484) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of C-dots. (B) Histogram analysis to determine the particle size distribution.
XPS spectra of C-dots. (A) Full-scan XPS spectrum. (B–D) High-resolution XPS fitting results for C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s show various functional groups on the surface of C-dots.

(A) UV–vis absorbance (black line), excitation (red line), and emission (broken yellow line) spectra of C-dots. (B) Emission spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelengths. (C) FT-IR spectrum of the prepared C-dots.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots after the treatment with different pollutants. (B) Bar graph representing the fluorescence intensity of C-dots with different pollutants. (C) Daylight (top row) and fluorescence (bottom row) images of C-dots after incubation with different pollutants.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots with different concentrations of 2-NP. (B) Digital fluorescence images of C-dots with varied concentrations of 2-NP from left to right.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots with different concentrations of 4-NP. (B) Digital fluorescence images of C-dots with varied concentrations of 4-NP from left to right taken under UV light.
Linear plots of C-dots’ fluorescence intensities (I0/I) and concentration of (A) 2-NP and (B) 4-NP.
Interference study of C-dots’ fluorescence response in the presence of a mixture of non-quenchers with (A) 2-NP and (B) 4-NP.
Changes in UV–vis absorbance spectra of C-dots in presence of (A) 2-NP, (B) 4-NP, and (C) 3-NP. Fluorescence decay of C-dots before and after the addition of (D) 2-NP and (E) 4-NP.
Diagrammatic representation for the synthetic process of yellow fluorescent C-dots and detection of 2-NP and 4-NP.
A comparison study of 2-NP and 4-NP detection using different C-dots.

row.entry:
Urea and ethylene
diamine tetraacetic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of 2-NP and 4-NP using C-dots in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots as selective nanoprobe for detection of 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Different types of phenolic pollutants pose a major risk to the environment and the health of living things. It is therefore crucial for their selective recognition especially nitrophenols. In this work, yellow fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were used as selective nanoprobe to efficiently detect 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). One-pot synthetic method was adopted for making C-dots from o-phenylenediamine and oleic acid mixture. The synthesized C-dots showed an excitation wavelength of 420 nm with Stokes shift of up to 135 nm. These C-dots exhibited instantaneous selective detection of 2-NP and 4-NP in aqueous solution with a detection limit of 6.4 nM and 4.8 nM respectively. Upon incubated with 2-NP and 4-NP, the C-dots’ fluorescence underwent quenching with a maximum extent of respective 80 % and 83 %. By comparing with other analogs of analyte, it was discovered that the emission intensity of the C-dots remained unchanged. Static quenching was proposed behind the changes of fluorescence after the treatment with 2-NP and 4-NP. Such type of C-dots will demonstrate the selective sensing platform in real field application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenols are widely used as the intermediate compound in the manufacture of drugs, pharmaceuticals, dyes, fungicides, and pesticides [1–3]. Release of these compounds into the environment has a great impact on the research society due to their hazardous health effects. United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) recognized that the 4-nitrophenol is the priority pollutant among the other organic contaminants because of its adverse effects towards human health [4]. The maximum allowance level of 2-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol in drinking water is about 4.8 µg/L and 60 μg/L [5,6]. The accumulation of nitrophenols in water is much easier due to the presence of hydroxyl groups which helps in greater solubility [7]. Presence of 2-NP and 4-NP in freshwater are highly toxic and cause headache and damage to the kidney, liver, and central nervous system [8]. It is very difficult to separate these contaminants from the wastewater due to their high stability and low biodegradability [9]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and effective technique to detect these pollutants from the environment. To date, different techniques for the detection of nitrophenol have been developed, which include electrochemistry, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis, and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [10,11]. Although, the existing methods detect analytes in lower concentrations, they require skilled operators, long-time detection, and time consumption. Comparatively, fluorescence-based techniques have great attention because of their rapid detection, simplicity, high selectivity and sensitivity, and low cost [12,13]. Therefore, it is very desirable to create a fluorophore for the specific recognition of 2-NP and 4-NP.

A unique class of carbon-based nanomaterials known as carbon dots (C-dots) have intriguing characteristics such as chemical stability, photostability, and biocompatibility [14]. The C-dots are spherical in shape with a size of less than 10 nm [15,16]. C-dots are typically organized by surface functional groups and carbon core. Surface functionalities of C-dots play a vital role in the detection of analyte. When the functional moieties in the analytes get attracted and come closer to the C-dots functional groups, interaction takes place that leads to fluorescence changes [17]. Because of the luminescence properties of C-dots, they are employed as sensor in a wide range of applications such as selective pollutant sensing, drugs, metal ions detection, bio sensors, disease diagnosis, etc. [18–25]. Some of the works report the detection of 2-NP and 4-NP using C-dots [26–28]. For example, Blue emissive nitrogen doped C-dots were prepared by Liao et al. and detection of 4-NP was reported through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [26]. Blue fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorous-doped C-dots were used by Tu et al. for selective detection of 2,4-dinitrophenol and 4-NP [27]. C-dots’ fluorescence underwent quenching after the treatment of those analytes and inner filter effect (IFE) was explained as responsible factor for fluorescence quenching. Min et al. synthesized yellow emissive C-dots from o-phenylenediamine and dicyandiamine mixture to sense 4-NP in an aqueous medium [28]. Addition of 4-NP into C-dots solution resulted in fluorescence quenching and showed that it followed static quenching mechanism. But, lower wavelength emissive, low fluorescence quantum yield of C-dots, longer incubation time with analytes limit their usage. In addition, the utilization of low wavelength emissive C-dots is not favorable in biological applications. Therefore, the development of bright emissive C-dots with higher wavelengths for the instantaneous detection of 2-NP and 4-NP in aqueous medium is highly required.

To achieve this goal, we have synthesized bright yellow fluorescent C-dots with quantum yield of 19.5 % for instantaneous selective detection of 2-NP and 4-NP in aqueous medium. A simple carbonization approach was followed to synthesize C-dots from a combination of o-phenylenediamine and oleic acid. The resulting C-dots were subjected to extensive spectroscopic and microscopic analysis to determine the surface functional groups and morphology of these nanomaterials. The C-dots were employed as a novel sensing platform for the detection of phenolic compounds. Among the tested phenolic compounds, 2-NP and 4-NP exhibited remarkable selectivity by quenching of C-dots’ fluorescence. The other analytes showed very insignificant response even at high concentration. Static quenching was proposed and explained for the C-dots’ fluorescence quenching in case of 2-NP and 4-NP. These findings will enlarge the idea for bright and longer wavelength emissive C-dots for more potential applications.

o-Phenylenediamine (99 %) was purchased from SD Fine Chem Ltd. Dimethyl Formamide (99 %) (DMF) and 4-iodophenol (99 %) (4-IP) were purchased from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd. Oleic acid (65–88 %) was purchased from Merck Life Science Pvt Ltd. 2-nitrophenol (99 %) (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (99 %) (3-NP), 4-nitrophenol (99 %) (4-NP), 4-aminophenol (98 %) (4-AP), 4-chorophenol (98 %) (4-CP), 4-bromophenol (99 %) (4-BP), and phenol (99 %) were purchased from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd. Resorcinol (99 %) (RES), Picric acid (99.5–100 %) (PA) and 2, 4-dinitrophenol (99 %) (DNP) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. Hexane (97 %) was purchased from Finar Chemicals.

C-dots were synthesized via one-pot carbonization method. Briefly, 100 mg of o-phenylenediamine and 1 mL of oleic acid were added in 3 mL of DMF in a glass vial. Then, the mixture was sonicated to obtain a homogeneous solution. Next, the mixed solution was heated at 200 °C for 2 h on a magnetic stirrer hot plate. A color change from yellow to brown was observed stipulating the formation of C-dots. Next, the C-dots were purified by using hexane as a solvent via the precipitation method. The synthesized product was dissolved in deionized water and the resultant solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate the impurities. The obtained supernatant was preserved and utilized as the stock solution for further measurements.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra were measured by Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer. Synergy H1-H1M multimode microplate reader was used to measure the fluorescence measurement. SHIMADZU IRAffinity-1 spectrophotometer was used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to evaluate the size and shape of C-dots by utilizing FEI Tecnai 20 ST instrument. The D2 phaser desktop/max-RAX-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to measure the X-ray diffraction (XRD) from 10 to 90° with Cu Ka radiation at 30 kV accelerating voltage and 10 mA current. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed by KALPHA- M/s Thermo Fisher Scientific instruments (UK). Horiba Yobin’s (DeltaFlex-01) time-correlated single-photon counting spectrometer was used to measure C-dots’ fluorescence lifetime.

The quantum yield of C-dots was measured by using fluorescein dye as reference standard (QY = 79 % with an excitation wavelength of 425 nm) by using the following formula
QY
sam
=
QY
ref
PL area
/ OD
sam
PL area
/ OD
ref
η
sam
2
η
ref
2
where, sam indicates the sample, ref denotes standard reference, η is solvent refractive index, PLarea is fluorescence area, and OD indicates absorbance value.

To detect the analytes, a stock solution of C-dots (concentration of 6.6 µg/mL) aqueous dispersion was firstly prepared. Varied concentrations of 2-NP and 4-NP aqueous solutions were also prepared. Different concentrations of 2-NP and 4-NP ranging from 0.74-134 µM were incubated with 2 mL of C-dots solution and fluorescence spectra were measured instantaneously. An excitation wavelength of 420 nm was used to measure emission spectrum of C-dots prior to and after addition of analytes. The higher concentration (134 µM) of other analytes such as phenol, 3-NP, 4-AP, 4-BP, 4-CP, 4-IP, RES, DNP, and PA were added to the 2 mL of the C-dots solution for comparison study.

A simple carbonization method was used to prepare the yellow fluorescent C-dots. Firstly, o-phenylenediamine (100 mg) and oleic acid (1 mL) were taken in a vial and DMF was used as a solvent to dissolve the mixture. The solution in the vial was heated at 200 °C for 2 h on a magnetic stirrer hot plate. The obtained brown-colored solution indicates the formation of C-dots. The C-dots were cooled at room temperature and purified by using hexane as a solvent via precipitation method. After adding deionized water to the product, it exhibited bright yellow fluorescence when exposed to UV light. The bright yellow fluorescence was dramatically quenched when 2-NP and 4-NP were treated with C-dots solution. The C-dots synthetic procedure and the fluorometric recognition of 2-NP and 4-NP have been represented in Scheme 1 . Carbonaceous precursors form carbogenic core during heating followed by the nucleation in prolonged heating resulting in the formation of C-dots [29]. The generation of the yellow fluorescent C-dots depends on the degree of conjugation with sp2 and sp3 and defect sites [30]. Doping of heteroatoms in the carbon matrix not only contributes to an increase in the solubility of C-dots in polar solvents but also results in diverse structural defects with varying energy levels [31,32]. Herein, the yellow luminous C-dots were obtained by nitrogen and oxygen-containing C-dots precursors via the carbonization process.

The morphology of the generated C-dots was analyzed using TEM. The size of the particles was less than 10 nm with a spherical form (Fig. 1 A) and the average diameter was found to be 2.5 ± 1.2 nm using histogram analysis (Fig. 1B).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to assess the chemical bonding and the existence of functional groups in the synthesized C-dots’ surface. Fig. 2 A illustrates the survey spectrum of C-dots containing C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. Their amounts were recorded as 58.3 %, 7 %, and 20.42 % for carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen respectively. The deconvoluted spectra of C 1s in Fig. 2B dissociated into two peaks at the range of 284.5 eV and 286.2 eV representing the CC/CC and CO/CN groups respectively [33]. The peaks at the region of 399 eV and 400.2 eV represent the CNC and CNH groups as observed in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s was deconvoluted into two peaks at the region of 532.2 eV and 533.1 eV corresponding to CO and CO groups respectively as shown in Fig. 2D [35].

UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra were used to demonstrate the optical properties of C-dots. The C-dots exhibit high absorption peaks at 245 nm and 420 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions, which were attributed to the CC and CN/CO functional moieties present on the surface of the C-dots as shown in Fig. 3 A [12]. The emission spectrum showed maximum intensity at 555 nm when C-dots were excited at wavelength of 420 nm. Absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra of C-dots have been shown in Fig. 3A. The maximum emission intensity at 420 nm in excitation spectrum coincides with C-dots absorption band. The C-dots aqueous dispersion when excited between 350 nm and 500 nm wavelengths with an interval of 50 nm, no changes in the maximum emission intensity wavelength position were observed which denotes the excitation-independent emission property. This specifies that C-dots are uniformly distributed with narrow energy suface state [36].

The fluorescence quantum yield of synthesized C-dots was measured using fluorescein dye as a standard and the value was calculated as 19.5 %. It designates bright fluorescent C-dots were formed. X-ray diffraction pattern of prepared C-dots was measured and one peak appeared at 21.7° in Fig. S1 (Supporting information) corresponds to graphitic structure interplanar distance [37]. These characterizations confirm the formation of C-dots. FT-IR data was analyzed to determine the surface functional groups on the C-dots (Fig. 3C). Transmittance peaks at 3499 cm−1 and 3230 cm−1 correspond to OH and NH groups [11,36]. The CH bond appeared in the range of 2935 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the CO groups appeared in the region of 1659 cm−1 [38]. The distinctive absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 correspond to CC (aromatic)/CN groups [39]. The two peaks at 1232 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 were denoted to CO stretching [40]. The peaks at 745 cm−1 and 655 cm−1 correspond to –NH2 wagging bands. The prepared yellow C-dots were exposed to a tungsten lamp of 150 W for 1 h to test the photostability. Samples were taken at various time intervals during the irradiation process. Fig. S2A (Supporting information) shows no discernible changes in C-dots' emission intensity indicating good photostability. Fluorescence digital images were also captured as shown in Fig. S2B (Supporting information). No obvious reduction of C-dots' emission brightness was observed even after 1 h light irradiation. This substantiates that the prepared C-dots are resistant against photobleaching.

The stability of synthesized C-dots at different pH was studied. Aqueous solution of C-dots was mixed with different pH solutions ranging from 3 to 11 and fluorescence was measured. As shown in Fig. S3A (Supporting information), C-dots’ emission intensity is comparatively lower in the case of acidic conditions and it remains higher in both the neutral as well as the basic conditions. Low fluorescence intensity in the case of acidic conditions might be due to the protonation of amine functional groups [13]. However, the deprotonation of the amine groups in the neutral and basic pH resulted in the restoration of fluorescence intensity. Hence, these fluorescent C-dots are capable to use in neutral and basic conditions. C-dots' fluorescence properties at different temperatures was studied and the result has been displayed in Fig. S3B (Supporting information). No fluorescence changes were observed for C-dots at different temperatures ranging between 20 °C and 60 °C with an interval of 10 °C. These outcomes indicate that the fluorescence intensity of C-dots remains stable even at higher temperature. The impact on C-dots fluorescence properties against salt was studied. Different concentration of NaCl was added to the C-dots solution and fluorescence was measured. It can be seen from Fig. S3C (Supporting information) that no changes were observed for C-dots up to 7.5 mM NaCl incubation. Henceforth, it can be concluded that there is no influence of NaCl salt on C-dots fluorescence property. Stability of C-dots against storage days has been represented in Fig. S3D (Supporting information). The results imply that there is no change in the fluorescence property of C-dots up to 28 days storage, which states good stability of C-dots. Therefore, it can be concluded that these C-dots holds tremendous potential towards pH, temperature, NaCl salt concentration and storage.

One of the most crucial requirements for a successful nanoprobe is the ability towards selective detection. The fluorescence response of C-dots was examined after the treatment of different pollutants namely phenol, 2-NP, 4-NP, 3-NP, 4-AP, 4-BP, 4-CP, 4-IP and RES to assess the selectivity. A maximum concentration of 134 µM of analytes was separately added to the C-dots aqueous solution and the emission spectra of C-dots were recorded instantaneously at 420 nm excitation wavelength to check the fluorescence properties of C-dots. There was a significant quenching of C-dots' emission intensity upon the treatment with 2-NP and 4-NP as shown in Fig. 4 A. A maximum fluorescence quenching of 80 % and 83 % was observed for respective 2-NP and 4-NP treatment. Surprisingly, non-significant changes of C-dots intensity were observed for other analytes. In parallel to the monosubstituted phenolic compounds, di and tri-substituted phenolic compounds were also tested. DNP and PA were separately incubated with C-dots’ aqueous solution and the results showed respective 52 % and 69 % fluorescence quenching (Fig. S4, Supporting information). Maximum fluorescence intensity at 555 nm was recorded before and after the addition of analytes and has been plotted in Fig. 4B.

It indicates the high selectivity of the synthesized C-dots towards 2-NP and 4-NP. Digital images of pre-incubated and post-incubated C-dots solution were also captured and have been presented in Fig. 4C. Daylight images (top row) show the significant color changes after the addition of 2-NP and 4-NP. Fluorescence images (bottom row) were seized and dramatic reduction of C-dots’ emission brightness was noticed for those two pollutants. Surprisingly, other pollutants didn’t show any significant changes. Thus, it is possible to detect these two pollutants under daylight and fluorescence method using the synthesized C-dots. Next, different concentrations of 2-NP and 4-NP ranging from 0.74-134 µM were incubated with C-dots aqueous solution to measure the sensitivity and to calculate limit of detection (LOD). Figs. 5 and 6 represent the fluorescence intensity of C-dots against different concentrations of 2-NP and 4-NP. It was noted that the increment in the incubation of 2-NP and 4-NP concentration leads to a progressive decrease in the C-dots’ fluorescence intensity. It was further supported by capturing fluorescence digital images.

The values of LOD were measured by plotting the I0/I versus nitrophenol concentration. Linear graphs were observed for both the cases as shown in Fig. 7 A and B. An excellent correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9806 and 0.9862 was obtained from the plots. The values of LOD were calculated as 6.4 nM and 4.8 nM for respective 2-NP and 4-NP. The calculated LOD values are highly significant and in line with the suggested works of literature as shown in Table 1
[6,15,26,27,33,37,41–48].

Interference experiments were conducted to demonstrate the selectivity of the prepared C-dots towards 2-NP and 4-NP. Firstly, all the non-quencher analytes such as phenol, 3-NP, 4-AP, 4-BP, 4-CP, 4-IP, and RES were added together to the C-dots solution and fluorescence was measured. It was observed that the variation of C-dots’ emission intensity was negligible (Fig. 8 ). Next, 2-NP and 4-NP were invidually added to the solution and the emission spectra were recorded. The results demonstrate a dramatic decrease of emission intensity after the treatment of 2-NP and 4-NP. It validates that these C-dots selectively recognize these two pollutants in spite of the presence of other interferences.

Fluorescence quenching can be due to the several processes such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, IFE, and FRET. In case of static quenching, C-dots interact with analyte to generate ground-state, non-fluorescent complex [49]. For static quenching it is anticipated that 1) the addition of analyte does not significantly change the fluorescence lifetime of C-dots; 2) the complexation of C-dots with analyte will lead to the appearance of new absorption peak; 3) the temperature influence will be less prominent than dynamic quenching. Dynamic quenching will take place via collisional deactivation and there will be a significant change of C-dots’ fluorescence lifetime after the addition of analyte. IFE and FRET involve overlap between absorbance spectrum of quencher and emission spectrum of fluorophore [50]. Several experiments were conducted to confirm quenching mechanism based on these anticipated phenomena. There is no overlap of C-dots’ emission spectrum with absorbance spectrum of analytes revealing non-occurance of FRET and IFE in our study. Absorbance spectra of bare C-dots, analytes, and C-dots-analyte mixture were measured. Fig. 9 A-B displays the significant changes in the absorbance spectra for C-dots + 2-NP and C-dots + 4-NP compared to bare C-dots. Complex formation of C-dots with the analyte results in the formation of new peaks that differ from those of bare C-dots. Conversely, no changes were observed in the case of C-dots and 3-NP mixture (Fig. 9C).

The Fluorescence lifetime of C-dots was also measured before and after the addition of analytes (concentration of 134 µM). The decay of C-dots’ fluorescence has been presented in Fig. 9D and E. The lifetime value for bare C-dots was checked as 1.67 ns. After the treatment with 2-NP and 4-NP, the values were measured as 1.70 and 1.67 ns respectively with a very good fit (χ2 values ranging between 1.07 and 1.17). The change in C-dots’ fluorescence lifetime is insignificant in case of 2-NP. Table S1 (Supporting information) presents the lifetime and χ2 values for both 2-NP and 4-NP. The changes in absorbance spectra for complex formation and no change in fluorescence lifetime indicates the occurance of static quenching behind the C-dots' fluorescence quenching. To further validate the static quenching, temperature dependent fluorescence changes of C-dots and 4-NP mixture was studied. As shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting Information), no significant changes of C-dots’ fluorescence intensity were observed with increase in temperature from 20 °C to 60 °C. This result further supports the static quenching. The position of –NO2 and –OH groups in 2-NP and 4-NP makes the existence of active and more resonating structures due to the resonance effect. Those charged species can easily interact with C-dots’ functional groups and form a non-fluorescent complex.

To investigate the usefulness of these prepared C-dots, a proposed method was filed to detect 2-NP and 4-NP in three different types of water samples: drinking water, industrial water, and river water. Initially the water samples were filtered using syringe filter with pore diameter of 0.2 μm. Certain concentration (0.06 µM) of 2-NP and 4-NP were spiked into 2 mL of real samples solution containing C-dots. Then, the fluorescence was measured instantaneously. Following the addition of 2-NP and 4-NP, the recovery rate was calculated from each water sample and it was ranged from 96.7 % to 106.7 % with a reasonable relative standard deviation as shown in Table 2 . The various contaminants in the water samples did not affect the detection medium's ability to identify 2-NP and 4-NP. These findings indicate that the approach has a promising future for use in environmental application.

In summary, we have developed a simple carbonization method for the synthesis of bright yellow fluorescent C-dots using o-phenylenediamine and oleic acid mixture. The C-dots showed optimal emission wavelength at 555 nm with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 19.5 %. The resulting fluorescent probes were effectively implemented for the selective detection of 2-NP and 4-NP in aqueous medium. Fluorescence intensity of C-dots became quenched upon the addition of 2-NP and 4-NP individually to the prepared C-dots solution. The fluorescence alteration showed good linearity with LOD values of 6.4 nM and 4.8 nM for 2-NP and 4-NP respectively. The mechanism of fluorescence decrement was identified as static quenching which was validated by time-correlated single-photon counting spectroscopy, UV–visible absorbance, and temperature-dependent studies. The C-dots also showed exceptional recovery of the analytes in various real water samples. As a result, this work offers a straightforward, user-friendly procedure that produces high-quality C-dots for environmental applications.

Indhumathi Ashokan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Susanta Kumar Bhunia: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Indhumathi A. acknowledges VIT University for providing fellowship and seed money as research grant. S. K. Bhunia expresses his appreciation to “VIT SEED GRANT” and DST INSPIRE funding (DST/ INSPIRE Faculty Award/2018/DST/INSPIRE/04/2018/002484) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ica.2024.122312.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

N.T.N. Anh, R.-a. Doong, ACS Applied Nano Materials, 1 (2018) 2153-2163.
